channel.title: NYT > Business > Energy & Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Western States Need Water. San Diego Has Extra. Will They Make A Deal?
item.description: San Diego County is shopping a surplus of desalinated seawater to Western states that are facing increasingly urgent drought and short supplies.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: A view of San Diego and the Pacific Ocean from Cabrillo National Monument.

item.title: Amit Forlit, Accused of Hacking Climate Activists, Appears in Court
item.description: Amit Forlit is accused of running a hacking operation that aimed to thwart climate litigation at the behest of a lobbying firm working for an oil giant.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal District Court in New York City. Amit Forlit has been indicted on charges of conspiracy to commit computer hacking, conspiracy to commit wire fraud and wire fraud.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall Sharply After Iran Says Strait of Hormuz Is Open
item.description: But analysts said it was not clear how quickly the oil industry in the Persian Gulf would be able to get back to normal.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: How Can I Protect My Car When the Weather Is Scorching?
item.description: Extreme heat is extremely hard on your vehicle, but a few simple precautions can help a lot.
item.dc:creator: Susan Shain

item.title: The Long-Term Plan to Scrub Carbon From the Sky
item.description: Microsoft is pulling back from efforts to remove carbon from the atmosphere. But the nascent industry’s proponents say they are thinking in decades, not years.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: A carbon capture facility in Tracy, Calif., in 2023.

item.title: Court Rejects Trump Administration Climate Lawsuit Against Hawaii
item.description: In a setback for federal efforts to thwart climate litigation, the judge ruled that the suit, which tried to block the state from suing oil companies, was too speculative.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Phillip Jung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. District Court building in Honolulu. Senior Judge Helen Gillmor dismissed the Justice Department’s case with prejudice, meaning it cannot be refiled.

item.title: Carbon Removal Industry Reels as Microsoft Retreats
item.description: Once held up as a key solution to climate change, a field that aims to remove carbon from the atmosphere is struggling to catch on.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Ariana Gomez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers at a direct air capture facility under construction in Texas in 2024. Hundreds of start-ups developing carbon removal technologies have raised more than $5 billion in recent years.

item.title: Middle East War Will Slow Global Economic Growth, I.M.F. Warns
item.description: The conflict could also fuel another bout of inflation, according to the International Monetary Fund.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: New International Monetary Fund projections showed a global economy that was stopped in its tracks by the U.S. war in Iran.

item.title: Trump’s Latest Oil Blockade Brings Bigger Economic Risks
item.description: Oil markets shrugged it off, but the effort to hurt Iran could provoke retaliation that inflicts more damage on energy assets and the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A strike on the Bapco oil refinery in Bahrain in early March. A U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz may give Iran a reason to restart attacks on energy assets throughout the Persian Gulf.

item.title: Youths Who Sued Trump Over Orders to ‘Unleash’ Energy Try to Revive Case
item.description: Appealing the dismissal of their suit, they argued that executive orders to promote fossil fuels endangered their futures and violated their constitutional rights.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Amanda Lucier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Julia Olson, a lawyer for the plaintiffs, arriving at court on Monday.

item.title: Iran Blockade Sets Up a Test of Which Side Can Endure More Pain
item.description: President Trump is trying to choke off the country’s lifeline with a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. But the Iranians are betting that his tolerance for political pain is limited.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fuel prices reflected on a semi-truck in Aurora, Ore., last week. “Soon you’ll be nostalgic for $4 to $5 gas,” Iran’s top negotiator warned American consumers after peace talks fell apart over the weekend.

item.title: Gray Whales Are Dying in San Francisco Bay
item.description: The animals might be entering the Bay in search of food as climate change disrupts traditional sources. They face huge risks from ships in the area.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A dead gray whale washed up at Point Reyes Station, Calif., in 2019.

item.title: China’s Electrostate Is Poised to Win From War in the Middle East
item.description: A cluster of Chinese firms is poised to win big from the energy crisis set off by the war in the Middle East, building on booming investment in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Keith Bradsher
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: An electricity transmission hub is at the terminus in south-central China of the world’s longest and most powerful ultrahigh-voltage power line. Chinese companies dominate the manufacturing of nearly every piece of a modern grid.

item.title: The Northeast Hoped to Lead on Climate. Now It’s Rethinking.
item.description: Many blue states are rethinking ambitious strategies to cut emissions as they struggle with rising electricity costs and new hurdles for renewable energy.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: Wind turbines intended for the South Fork offshore wind farm at State Pier in New London, Conn., in 2023.

item.title: Why Investing in Wind and Solar to Avoid Gas Shocks Hasn’t Added Up for Some
item.description: Renewable energy is cheaper to run than fossil fuels, especially with war choking oil supply. But it hasn’t turned out that way for some European countries, and the reason is complex.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown and Mira Rojanasakul
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Iran War Drives Deeper Oil Shock Than Prices Reveal
item.description: The war with Iran is preventing huge amounts of oil from flowing out of the Persian Gulf, but the prices that many people track don’t fully capture the scale of the disruption.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: E.P.A. Says It Will End Biden’s Coal Ash Disposal Rules
item.description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, said the changes would help U.S. “energy dominance.” Environmentalists said they threaten drinking water.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: The aftermath of a coal ash spill after a retention pond wall collapsed in Harriman, Tenn., in 2008.

item.title: Climate Change Denial Sees a Resurgence in Trump’s Washington
item.description: A conference near the White House drew hundreds of people who reject the scientific consensus on climate change. The mood was triumphant.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: William Happer, a Princeton professor emeritus of physics who has argued that global warming is good for humanity, during a presentation at the Heartland Institute’s International Climate Change Conference in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: How War in the Middle East Paralyzed an Asian Food Giant
item.description: Vietnam, the world’s No. 2 rice exporter, cut production as power prices surged. Even with a temporary cease-fire in Iran, worries linger over the world’s food supply.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Linh Pham
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A boat transporting newly harvested rice in My Thanh Ward, Dong Thap Province, Vietnam, in late March.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall, but Energy Firms Remain Frozen After U.S.-Iran Deal
item.description: Analysts said oil and natural gas energy companies would not quickly restore production unless attacks stopped and ships started moving through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Tankers anchored last month off Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz. Until shipping traffic starts moving again, oil and gas companies are not going to reopen the taps.
